prompt: In ecology, how do birds achieve precise location and direction navigation during migration? What cues and disturbances influence this process?
article:
# How Migratory Birds Navigate: Direction, Location, Cues, Disturbances, and Open Questions

## Assumptions, scope, and definitions

- This report focuses on **migratory birds** in an ecological and ornithological context, emphasizing how they solve two distinct problems: **choosing and maintaining direction** and **determining geographic position relative to a goal** [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7618438/)[[149]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-025-00543-8).
- I use **historical landmark experiments** to explain concepts, but the synthesis prioritizes **current scientific understanding through May 2026** [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[121]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574954125002274)[[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10589677/)[[128]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42003-023-04530-w).
- I treat navigation as **multimodal and context dependent**: birds do not use one universal cue, but shift among magnetic, celestial, visual, olfactory, and atmospheric information depending on species, life stage, route, weather, and familiarity [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[33]](https://www.bio.psy.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/bpsy/mam/content/papers/birdnav16.pdf)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/).
- I distinguish **well-supported findings** from **active hypotheses or contested mechanisms**, especially for magnetic map mechanisms, olfactory generality across taxa, and infrasound/low-frequency cues [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[32]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12365788/)[[100]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6769297/)[[111]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4203629/)[[115]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/ecology-and-evolution/articles/10.3389/fevo.2021.740027/full).

### Brief glossary

- **Compass sense**: ability to derive directional reference from the environment, such as from the Sun, stars, or Earth’s magnetic field [[150]](https://www.britannica.com/science/migration-animal/Navigation-and-orientation)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[149]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-025-00543-8).
- **Compass orientation**: using a compass sense to choose and maintain a heading; this solves direction but not necessarily location [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[150]](https://www.britannica.com/science/migration-animal/Navigation-and-orientation)[[102]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1664630/).
- **Map sense / positional map**: information that allows a bird to estimate where it is relative to a goal, often framed as the “map” step in a map-and-compass system [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7618438/)[[102]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1664630/).
- **True navigation**: reaching or orienting toward a known destination after displacement to an unfamiliar place using cues available there, implying both a map and a compass [[150]](https://www.britannica.com/science/migration-animal/Navigation-and-orientation)[[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7618438/)[[156]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33581072/).
- **Vector navigation / inherited migratory program**: following inherited direction-and-time or direction-and-distance instructions without a full positional map, common in first migrations [[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full)[[153]](https://www.internationalornithology.org/PROCEEDINGS_Durban/Symposium/S17/S17.1.htm).
- **Recalibration**: updating one compass relative to another, often at twilight when polarized light, Sun position, stars, and magnetic cues can be cross-referenced [[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/)[[158]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/behavioral-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnbeh.2016.00029/full)[[159]](https://lup.lub.lu.se/search/files/2905601/625386.pdf).
- **Displacement**: natural or experimental movement away from the expected route or location; can be physical or virtual (e.g., magnetic displacement) [[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full)[[156]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33581072/)[[157]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6086908/).
- **Piloting**: guidance by familiar landmarks or learned local routes rather than long-range positional mapping [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[114]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi).
- **Cue conflict**: mismatch between cues, natural or experimental, such as a shifted magnetic field paired with normal sunset cues [[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/)[[158]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/behavioral-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnbeh.2016.00029/full)[[159]](https://lup.lub.lu.se/search/files/2905601/625386.pdf).

## Executive summary

Bird migration navigation is best understood as a **two-part problem**. First, a bird must determine **direction**—which way to fly now. Second, it must sometimes determine **location**—where it is relative to a breeding site, wintering area, stopover, or migratory corridor [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7618438/)[[149]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-025-00543-8). These are not the same ability. A bird can maintain a seasonally appropriate heading using a compass without knowing it has been blown or displaced off course; conversely, true navigation requires some map-like estimate of position plus a compass to act on it [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/)[[156]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33581072/). That distinction explains why **juvenile white-crowned sparrows displaced 3,700 km** continued a seasonally appropriate southerly program while adults reoriented toward expected wintering areas, and why **adult starlings** in Perdeck’s classic work compensated while juveniles largely did not [[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/)[[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11285401/)[[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full).

For **direction finding**, the strongest evidence supports multiple compass systems: a **time-compensated sun compass**, a **learned star compass**, a **magnetic inclination compass**, and **polarized-light-based calibration**, especially at sunrise and sunset [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[13]](https://doi.org/10.1038/364523a0)[[33]](https://www.bio.psy.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/bpsy/mam/content/papers/birdnav16.pdf)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[39]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6936046/)[[42]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1513391113)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/). For **location finding**, the evidence is more heterogeneous. Some species, especially experienced adults, can compensate after large displacement, implying a positional map. Candidate map cues include **geomagnetic parameters** such as inclination, intensity, and declination, **olfactory landscapes**, and **learned visual/topographic information**, with strongest map evidence in some model systems such as Eurasian reed warblers, pigeons, and several seabirds [[17]](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-30477-8)[[18]](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2024.1363)[[50]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11512678/)[[53]](https://fr.scribd.com/document/376178988/Gagliardo-2013-Forty-years-of-bird-olfactory-navigation-pdf)[[68]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-30477-8)[[74]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-29919-0)[[79]](https://www.nature.com/articles/srep37326).

Birds appear to integrate cues **hierarchically and opportunistically**, not rigidly. Twilight often serves as a calibration window; celestial cues may dominate when visible, magnetic cues often serve as all-weather backup, olfaction and landmarks become more important in certain species or familiar regions, and wind/weather information shapes departure, drift compensation, stopover, and barrier decisions throughout the trip [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[37]](https://www.encyclopedie-environnement.org/en/life/orientation-migratory-birds/)[[42]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1513391113)[[44]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3049006/)[[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z)[[59]](https://lucris.lub.lu.se/ws/files/4386940/624645.pdf)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/). Juveniles and adults frequently differ: first-time migrants often rely more on inherited vector programs, while experienced adults show better displacement compensation, wind-drift correction, and site fidelity [[21]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12037376/)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/)[[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11285401/)[[84]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9581414/).

Navigation is altered or disrupted by both **natural variation** and **human disturbance**. Natural factors include cloud cover, crosswinds, storms, geomagnetic disturbance, smoke, and seasonally shifting resource distributions [[20]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-26586-0)[[37]](https://www.encyclopedie-environnement.org/en/life/orientation-migratory-birds/)[[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z)[[65]](https://www.audubon.org/news/new-study-first-explore-how-wildfire-smoke-derails-bird-migration)[[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10589677/). Anthropogenic factors include **artificial light at night**, **electromagnetic noise**, **urbanization and habitat change**, and **climate change** [[10]](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13290)[[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[179]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44284-025-00388-7)[[190]](https://newsmediarelations.colostate.edu/2019/12/16/bird-migration-timing-skewed-by-climate-new-research-finds/). These can act by **blocking cues** (e.g., clouds obscuring stars), **distorting cue reliability** (e.g., geomagnetic storms), **interfering with sensory processing** (e.g., radiofrequency noise affecting magnetic orientation), or **changing the ecological context in which navigation is expressed** (e.g., urban skyglow redistributing stopovers, habitat loss removing critical landfall sites) [[20]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-26586-0)[[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[184]](https://restoreactscienceprogram.noaa.gov/projects/migratory-birds)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/). The result may be anything from short-term cue switching to persistent disorientation, vagrancy, delayed migration, higher energetic cost, or collision mortality [[20]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-26586-0)[[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0006320725006573)[[75]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9829733/)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/). Concrete examples include a **9–17% decrease in migration intensity** during severe space weather over the U.S. Great Plains [[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10589677/), **more than 40,000** dead birds recorded over decades at Chicago’s McCormick Place [[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/), and Gulf-crossing studies in which about **75%** of tracked birds approaching the Gulf retreated inland or detoured rather than immediately crossing under unfavorable conditions [[188]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/retreat-detour-or-advance-understanding-movements-birds-confronting-gulf-mexico).

The central modern conclusion is therefore not that birds possess one “migration sense,” but that migration precision emerges from **redundant, interacting sensory systems**, combined with **internal timing**, **learning**, and **ecological decision-making** across trip phases [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[33]](https://www.bio.psy.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/bpsy/mam/content/papers/birdnav16.pdf)[[121]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574954125002274).

---

## Why bird migration navigation is a two-part problem: direction vs location

A bird that can keep flying southwest in autumn is solving a different problem from a bird that can infer it has been blown 1,000 km east of its usual route and then steer back toward its migratory corridor [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[68]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-30477-8)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/)[[156]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33581072/). The first is **orientation**; the second is **positional navigation** [[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7618438/)[[149]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-025-00543-8). This distinction is not semantic. It changes how experiments are interpreted:

- **Orientation tests** ask whether a bird can maintain a bearing relative to a reference system.
- **Navigation tests** ask whether a bird can infer its own displacement relative to a destination and then choose a compensatory heading [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7618438/)[[149]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-025-00543-8).

A first-time migrant using an inherited program can therefore appear “successful” over broad geographic scales without possessing a true map. That is often enough for initial autumn migration, especially in solitary night-migratory passerines [[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full)[[153]](https://www.internationalornithology.org/PROCEEDINGS_Durban/Symposium/S17/S17.1.htm). But it becomes insufficient when birds are **experimentally displaced**, strongly drifted by winds, or must return precisely to a previously known breeding or wintering site [[21]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12037376/)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/)[[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11285401/).

### Direction navigation vs location navigation

| Aspect | Direction navigation / compass orientation | Location navigation / map sense / true navigation |
|---|---|---|
| Core question | “Which way should I fly?” [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[102]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1664630/) | “Where am I relative to my goal?” [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7618438/)[[102]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1664630/) |
| Information required | A directional reference only: magnetic field, Sun, stars, polarized light [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[33]](https://www.bio.psy.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/bpsy/mam/content/papers/birdnav16.pdf)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/) | Spatial information about current position relative to destination or corridor [[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7618438/)[[149]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-025-00543-8)[[156]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33581072/) |
| Can work without prior experience? | Often yes, especially in juveniles using inherited vector programs [[21]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12037376/)[[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full)[[153]](https://www.internationalornithology.org/PROCEEDINGS_Durban/Symposium/S17/S17.1.htm) | Usually stronger in experienced adults; evidence for full juvenile map use is limited and debated [[21]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12037376/)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/)[[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11285401/)[[79]](https://www.nature.com/articles/srep37326) |
| Typical experimental tests | Emlen funnels, planetarium studies, clock-shift, magnetic rotations [[3]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5513225/)[[7]](https://www.shaverscreek.org/2015/06/09/marvel-of-migration-navigation-and-orientation/)[[11]](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF01952166)[[13]](https://doi.org/10.1038/364523a0)[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emlen_funnel) | Physical displacement, virtual magnetic displacement, anosmia, tracking of compensatory routes [[17]](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-30477-8)[[18]](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2024.1363)[[22]](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.avrs.2025.100285)[[68]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-30477-8)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/) |
| Main cues | Magnetic compass, sun compass, star compass, polarized light calibration [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[33]](https://www.bio.psy.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/bpsy/mam/content/papers/birdnav16.pdf)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[42]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1513391113) | Magnetic gradients/declination, olfactory landscapes, landmarks/topography, learned route context [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[18]](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2024.1363)[[50]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11512678/)[[74]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-29919-0)[[114]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi) |
| Failure mode if disrupted | Misorientation or inability to keep a stable heading [[10]](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13290)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d)[[89]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4801848/) | Failure to compensate for displacement, vagrancy, route shift, wrong landfall, missed goal area [[20]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-26586-0)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/)[[75]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9829733/) |
| Representative evidence | Clock-shifted pigeons change initial bearing in predictable ways; star-compass learning in indigo buntings; magnetic compass in robins [[7]](https://www.shaverscreek.org/2015/06/09/marvel-of-migration-navigation-and-orientation/)[[11]](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF01952166)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[39]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6936046/) | Adult but not juvenile white-crowned sparrows compensate after 3,700 km displacement; reed warblers compensate for real and virtual magnetic displacement [[17]](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-30477-8)[[68]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-30477-8)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/) |

### Worked comparison: one bird can pass the compass test and fail the map test

A useful end-to-end example is the age contrast in displacement experiments [69][70][152]:

1. **Input condition:** both juvenile and adult birds possess seasonally appropriate migratory motivation and at least one functioning compass.
2. **Manipulation:** birds are displaced far from the normal route.
3. **Observed juvenile response:** continue the inherited seasonally appropriate direction.
4. **Observed adult response:** reorient toward the expected goal region.
5. **Inference:** both groups can still solve the **direction** problem, but only adults show strong evidence of solving the **location** problem [69][70][152].

That logic is why displacement studies are central to claims about true navigation.

### Conceptual framework diagram

```text
                    MIGRATORY NAVIGATION IN BIRDS

        ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │                INTERNAL STATE / TIMING             │
        │ circannual program • circadian clock • motivation │
        └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
        ┌──────────────────────────────┐
        │      COMPASS SYSTEMS         │
        │ magnetic • sun • stars •     │
        │ polarized-light calibration  │
        └──────────────────────────────┘
                              │
             gives heading    │    recalibrated at twilight / experience
                              ▼
        ┌──────────────────────────────┐
        │      MAP / POSITIONING       │
        │ magnetic signatures?         │
        │ olfactory map? landmarks?    │
        │ route memory / signposts?    │
        └──────────────────────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
        ┌──────────────────────────────┐
        │   LOCAL GUIDANCE / PILOTING  │
        │ coastlines • rivers • urban/ │
        │ rural stopovers • familiar   │
        │ landmarks • final approach   │
        └──────────────────────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
        ┌──────────────────────────────┐
        │ REALIZED MOVEMENT OUTCOME    │
        │ departure • en route heading │
        │ stopover • barrier crossing  │
        │ correction • arrival         │
        └──────────────────────────────┘
```

**Interpretation:** compass systems solve direction; map systems solve position; local guidance refines movement near familiar or ecologically constrained areas. Cue weighting changes by phase, weather, and experience [1][2][33][72][184].

---

## Overview of migratory navigation across trip phases

Bird navigation is not one decision repeated continuously; it is a sequence of tasks that changes from predeparture calibration to final approach [1][33][184]. A useful mechanistic partition is:

- **Long-distance orientation**: often more than about **200 km** from the final goal, where global cues such as magnetic and celestial references dominate [33].
- **Narrowing-in phase**: roughly **1–200 km** from the goal, where learned local gradients, familiar habitat structure, and route memory become more important [33].
- **Pinpointing / final approach**: often within about **1 km**, where exact site recognition and local landmarks likely dominate [33].

This phase structure matters because it implies that asking “what cue do birds use?” is often the wrong question. The more precise question is **which cue does this species use at this phase, under these sensory conditions, with this degree of familiarity?** [1][33].

### Migration-phase workflow

```text
1. Premigratory preparation
   - photoperiod / internal clocks set migratory state
   - young birds learn sky rotation; compasses are calibrated [33][35][164]

2. Departure
   - twilight provides sunset / polarized-light information
   - bird selects initial heading, often before full darkness [35][72][188]

3. En route travel
   - compass orientation maintains heading
   - winds, cloud, and geomagnetic conditions alter cue availability [1][37][58]

4. Stopover
   - birds assess habitat, food, safety, and weather windows
   - stopover distribution reflects habitat structure and broad-scale attraction/constraint [165][176][177]

5. Barrier crossing or detour
   - birds choose whether to cross ocean/desert or retreat/detour
   - decisions depend on fuel, weather, and route context [58][184][188]

6. Compensation / correction
   - if drifted or displaced, experienced birds may correct using map-like information
   - compensation may be immediate, delayed, partial, or absent [17][68][69]

7. Arrival / final approach
   - local landmarks, habitat recognition, and familiar-area cues become important
   - final approach is especially vulnerable to light traps and collision hazards [2][114][185]
```

### Internal timing is an enabling system, not just background physiology

Compass use depends on timing. A **sun compass** is impossible without circadian time compensation, and an inherited vector program is impossible without some seasonal schedule [33][35][153]. Garden warblers kept under constant daily light, constant temperature, and constant food still showed annual periodicity in migratory restlessness, molt, gonadal changes, and fattening, supporting an endogenous circannual program rather than simple immediate environmental triggering [33]. That does not tell birds where they are, but it helps determine **when to depart**, **which seasonal direction to prefer**, and **how long to maintain a programmed heading** [33][153].

### Why phases matter

- **Departure** is often a calibration window because sunset, polarized light, and emerging stars allow alignment among compasses [42][44][72].
- **En route flight** emphasizes compass maintenance and drift handling; cue availability changes with cloud, topography, and time of day [1][37][58].
- **Stopover** is both ecological and navigational: where a bird lands depends on weather, coastlines, habitat structure, food, and increasingly on anthropogenic lightscapes [[165]](https://www.fs.usda.gov/psw/publications/documents/psw_gtr191/psw_gtr191_0684-0689_moore.pdf)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[177]](https://acjv.org/radar_study/FinalReport_La_Puma&Buler_2013.pdf)[[178]](https://www.fws.gov/press-release/2018-04/new-study-uses-weather-radar-technology-identify-important-stopover-sites).
- **Barrier zones** such as the Gulf of Mexico, deserts, and coasts amplify the cost of navigational error, making wind support, stopover availability, and correction especially important [[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z)[[184]](https://restoreactscienceprogram.noaa.gov/projects/migratory-birds)[[188]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/retreat-detour-or-advance-understanding-movements-birds-confronting-gulf-mexico).
- **Final approach** may rely more on local guidance and familiar cues than on large-scale maps, and is where urban lights and glass become especially dangerous [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[114]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/).

## Direction finding (compass orientation): mechanisms and evidence

Direction finding is the most strongly supported part of bird migration navigation. Birds clearly possess several compass systems and can switch among them [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[33]](https://www.bio.psy.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/bpsy/mam/content/papers/birdnav16.pdf)[[39]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6936046/). The main unresolved questions concern weighting, calibration, and sensory transduction—not whether these compass systems exist [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/)[[100]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6769297/).

### Sun compass

Birds can use the Sun as a directional cue, but only if they know the **time of day**. Because solar azimuth shifts predictably through the day—about **15° per hour**—a functional sun compass must be **time compensated** by an internal circadian clock [[33]](https://www.bio.psy.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/bpsy/mam/content/papers/birdnav16.pdf)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Olfactory_navigation).

Classic **clock-shift** experiments in pigeons showed that shifting the birds’ internal clocks produced predictable directional errors at release, exactly as expected if they were using solar azimuth relative to internal time rather than simply flying toward a memorized sun position [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[11]](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF01952166)[[12]](https://doi.org/10.1242/jeb.199.1.21). Similar logic underlies sun-compass evidence in migratory songbirds and at least one pelagic seabird [[4]](https://journals.ansfoundation.org/index.php/jans/article/view/2684)[[23]](https://doi.org/10.1163/1568539X-00003560).

**What the sun compass provides:** directional heading.  
**What it does not provide by itself:** geographic position [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/).

### Star compass

Nocturnal migrants can orient by the night sky. The key breakthrough came from **planetarium experiments** with indigo buntings, which showed that young birds learn the **center of celestial rotation** rather than inheriting a fixed star atlas [[7]](https://www.shaverscreek.org/2015/06/09/marvel-of-migration-navigation-and-orientation/)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[164]](https://nationalzoo.si.edu/migratory-birds/news/stellar-migrant). Birds oriented randomly under a fixed star field lacking rotation, oriented appropriately under a normal rotating sky, and could later orient relative to an artificially imposed rotational center, showing that stellar north is learned developmentally from sky rotation [[7]](https://www.shaverscreek.org/2015/06/09/marvel-of-migration-navigation-and-orientation/)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[164]](https://nationalzoo.si.edu/migratory-birds/news/stellar-migrant).

This is a classic example of **innate capacity plus learned reference frame**: birds appear predisposed to attend to rotating star patterns, but must experience a normal rotating sky to calibrate the system [[33]](https://www.bio.psy.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/bpsy/mam/content/papers/birdnav16.pdf)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/). For **night migrants**, this system can provide a stable geographic frame when stars are visible. For **day migrants**, it is less directly usable during flight, but the developmental learning still matters for species that migrate partly or wholly at night [[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[80]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9532420/)[[81]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5989362/).

### Magnetic compass

The avian magnetic compass is one of the best established orientation systems. It was first demonstrated in migratory birds such as the European robin using controlled magnetic field manipulations [[10]](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13290)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[39]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6936046/). This compass is not like a human polarity compass. It is an **inclination compass**, sensitive to the angle of field lines relative to gravity, allowing birds to distinguish poleward from equatorward directions [[33]](https://www.bio.psy.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/bpsy/mam/content/papers/birdnav16.pdf)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[39]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6936046/)[[102]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1664630/).

Two mechanistic features are especially important:

- It is **light dependent**, with functional orientation under short wavelengths from ultraviolet into green, up to about **565 nm**, while yellow/red conditions can induce disorientation in the experimental literature summarized by reviews [[100]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6769297/)[[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5582129/).
- It operates within a **functional intensity window**, but birds can recalibrate if pre-exposed to fields outside the initial range, indicating a flexible rather than rigid tuning [[100]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6769297/)[[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5582129/).

This matters ecologically. A light-dependent magnetic compass can function when stars or the Sun are obscured, but it is also vulnerable to lighting conditions and electromagnetic interference in ways a purely mechanical compass would not be [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[37]](https://www.encyclopedie-environnement.org/en/life/orientation-migratory-birds/)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d)[[100]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6769297/).

#### Mechanistic evidence for magnetic compass sensing

- Cryptochromes, especially **Cry4/Cry4a**, remain leading molecular candidates, though not all Cry4 isoforms appear equally relevant, and exact photochemistry remains unresolved [[38]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-020-72579-2)[[132]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34163056/)[[134]](https://ifv-vogelwarte.de/en/research/projects/cryptochrome-adaptive-evolution-and-loss-of-a-putative-magnetoreceptor-in-birds).
- Magnetic compass information is processed through the **visual system** rather than the trigeminal system; studies cited in reviews report visual but not trigeminal mediation for compass information, and the forebrain region **Cluster N** is implicated in night-migratory magnetic orientation [[38]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-020-72579-2)[[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0959438812000074)[[89]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4801848/).
- The exact radical pair and transduction steps remain debated, especially because some theoretical models struggle to reproduce the sensitivity implied by behavioral radiofrequency effects [[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5582129/)[[131]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-55124-x)[[139]](https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:21628613-f9de-446c-9d6d-91ae8f045c30/files/m4e6e225e4912c08e48c31d2e329f96db).

### Polarized light

Birds also use the sky’s polarization pattern, especially around **sunrise and sunset**, primarily for **compass calibration** rather than as a stand-alone all-night compass [[13]](https://doi.org/10.1038/364523a0)[[42]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1513391113)[[44]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3049006/)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/). Access to the band of maximum polarization near the **horizon** appears especially important [[42]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1513391113)[[44]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3049006/)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/).

Experiments by Able and Able showed that access to natural skylight polarization is necessary for proper calibration of magnetic orientation during daylight [[13]](https://doi.org/10.1038/364523a0). Later work and syntheses argue that birds recalibrate the magnetic compass from twilight polarization if they can see the appropriate horizon region, and then may recalibrate the star compass relative to the newly calibrated magnetic reference [[42]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1513391113)[[44]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3049006/)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/).

### Day migrants vs night migrants in compass use

The major cue systems are shared, but their geometry differs:

- **Day migrants** can use the Sun and visible topography continuously during travel, and may rely less on stellar cues during active flight [[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[71]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5558239/)[[81]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5989362/).
- **Night migrants** often rely on **twilight** for calibration, **stars** when the sky is clear, and the **magnetic compass** when celestial cues are blocked [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/)[[80]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9532420/).

This is a probabilistic difference, not a rigid division. Night migrants still use topography near coasts or landfall, and day migrants also show magnetic orientation abilities [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z)[[81]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5989362/).

### Direction-finding summary

For compass orientation, the broad consensus is strong: birds can determine and maintain direction using **sun**, **stars**, and the **magnetic field**, with **polarized light** playing a major calibration role [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[33]](https://www.bio.psy.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/bpsy/mam/content/papers/birdnav16.pdf)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[42]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1513391113). The debate is no longer whether these systems exist, but **how they are weighted**, **how they interact**, and **how their sensory biology works in detail** [[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/)[[100]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6769297/)[[121]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574954125002274).

## Location finding (map sense / positional navigation): mechanisms and evidence

Location finding is the harder and more controversial problem. A bird that simply follows an inherited heading is not demonstrating a map; a bird that compensates after displacement is [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7618438/)[[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full). Here the literature is more species-specific and method-sensitive than it is for compass orientation [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[32]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12365788/)[[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7618438/).

### Map architectures: bi-gradient maps versus mosaic maps

Current reviews commonly distinguish two broad classes of positional information [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/):

- **Bi-gradient or coordinate-like maps**, which use large-scale spatial gradients such as magnetic **inclination**, **intensity**, or possibly **declination** [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[18]](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2024.1363)[[32]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12365788/).
- **Mosaic maps**, which use learned assemblages of landscape features, odor distributions, coastlines, topography, or route-specific signposts [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[50]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11512678/)[[74]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-29919-0)[[114]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi).

That distinction matters because not all “map” hypotheses imply an exact latitude-longitude estimate. An olfactory map, for example, may provide a **goalward directional estimate** from learned odor gradients rather than a strict two-coordinate fix [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[50]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11512678/)[[74]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-29919-0).

### Innate vector navigation versus true navigation

The traditional model for first-time migrants, especially solitary nocturnal passerines, is **vector navigation**: fly in direction A for a certain time, then direction B, and so on [[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full)[[153]](https://www.internationalornithology.org/PROCEEDINGS_Durban/Symposium/S17/S17.1.htm). This can generate a plausible first migration without requiring a true positional map [[153]](https://www.internationalornithology.org/PROCEEDINGS_Durban/Symposium/S17/S17.1.htm).

The strongest evidence for this comes from **age differences in displacement studies**. Perdeck’s classic starling experiment involved **11,247 common starlings** captured in the Netherlands and displaced to Switzerland between 1948 and 1957; displaced adults corrected toward northwestern Europe, whereas juveniles largely continued the normal southwest direction [[21]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12037376/)[[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11285401/). A later re-analysis concluded that the juvenile pattern could not be explained simply by copying local flocks [[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11285401/). Likewise, adult white-crowned sparrows displaced **3,700 km** across North America oriented west-southwest toward expected wintering areas, while juveniles continued in a more southerly innate direction [[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/).

These studies imply that **experience matters**: adults often possess learned positional information that juveniles lack [[21]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12037376/)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/)[[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11285401/).

### Magnetic map hypotheses

One major candidate for map sense is a **geomagnetic map** based on spatial variation in magnetic **inclination**, **intensity**, and possibly **declination** [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[32]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12365788/)[[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7618438/)[[156]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33581072/).

The strongest recent evidence comes from **Eurasian reed warblers**:

- Birds physically displaced longitudinally reoriented in a compensatory way rather than maintaining their original heading [[16]](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep16402)[[17]](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-30477-8)[[167]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8918449/).
- Birds exposed to **virtual magnetic displacement**—magnetic parameters changed while celestial, photoperiodic, visual, and olfactory cues remained unchanged—also reoriented as if physically displaced [[17]](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-30477-8)[[68]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-30477-8).
- In one key experiment, sham-operated reed warblers responded to a virtual **1,000 km** eastward displacement by shifting from local NE migratory direction toward NW, whereas birds with severed **ophthalmic trigeminal nerves** failed to compensate, showing that trigeminal input is necessary for this magnetic positioning response [[17]](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-30477-8)[[157]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6086908/).
- A 2024 study reported that reed warblers could extract positional information from **inclination plus declination alone**, even when total intensity was held at the physical-site value, suggesting that birds may not need all magnetic components equally [[18]](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2024.1363)[[32]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12365788/).

#### Worked example: what the reed warbler experiments actually show

The reed warbler work is one of the clearest executed tests of magnetic positional information [[17]](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-30477-8)[[18]](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2024.1363)[[68]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-30477-8)[[157]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6086908/):

1. **Constant nonmagnetic context:** same physical site, same local odors, same photoperiod, same stars and visual surroundings.
2. **Manipulated variable:** only the magnetic field parameters are changed inside experimental coils.
3. **Behavioral result in intact birds:** orientation shifts in the direction expected if the bird interprets itself as having been moved to a different geographic region.
4. **Behavioral result after trigeminal intervention:** compensatory shift disappears.
5. **Inference:** at least in this species and context, magnetic parameters contribute not just to compass heading but to **positional estimation** [[17]](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-30477-8)[[18]](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2024.1363)[[68]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-30477-8)[[157]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6086908/).

This is much stronger than a simple magnetic-compass result because the manipulation is designed to change **where the bird thinks it is**, not merely **which way north lies**.

#### Limits and debates on magnetic maps

This evidence is strong for one model system, but it does not mean all migratory birds use a universal magnetic bicoordinate map. Major limitations include:

- Some species do **not** show expected declination use or goalward compensation after displacement [[4]](https://journals.ansfoundation.org/index.php/jans/article/view/2684)[[79]](https://www.nature.com/articles/srep37326).
- Tracking-based syntheses argue that no GPS-based field dataset has yet definitively shown that a geomagnetic map explains general migration routes in the wild [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/).
- A 2023 critique of virtual magnetic displacement studies argued that many designs allow **multiple plausible “virtual locations”**, making some map interpretations ambiguous [[110]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9941108/)[[128]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42003-023-04530-w).
- In experienced **white-throated sparrows** displaced **2,200 km** west from southern Ontario to Saskatchewan, manipulations of magnetic and olfactory systems did not produce clear goalward compensation after release, underscoring that even experienced migrants do not always reveal an identifiable map mechanism under test conditions [[79]](https://www.nature.com/articles/srep37326).

So the current position is: **magnetic positional information is strongly supported in some species, but its generality, exact coordinates, and sensory implementation remain debated** [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[32]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12365788/)[[110]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9941108/)[[128]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42003-023-04530-w).

### Olfactory maps

Olfaction is one of the strongest nonmagnetic map candidates, especially in **homing pigeons** and several **seabirds** [[50]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11512678/)[[53]](https://fr.scribd.com/document/376178988/Gagliardo-2013-Forty-years-of-bird-olfactory-navigation-pdf)[[74]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-29919-0)[[111]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4203629/). The classic olfactory-map hypothesis proposes that birds learn the odor composition associated with winds at home and later infer displacement direction from atmospheric odor mixtures at unfamiliar sites [[50]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11512678/)[[51]](https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:e70968e1-1f63-4fba-ab24-fee69481f886/files/r2b88qd40r)[[52]](https://www.egu.eu/news/81/how-pigeons-may-smell-their-way-home/)[[170]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23720797/).

Evidence includes:

- Long-standing findings that **anosmic pigeons** often fail to orient homeward from unfamiliar sites [[53]](https://fr.scribd.com/document/376178988/Gagliardo-2013-Forty-years-of-bird-olfactory-navigation-pdf)[[170]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23720797/)[[171]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/1838523/).
- GPS and neurobiological work indicating that environmental odors at release sites can provide map-like information before departure [[50]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11512678/).
- Field studies on **Cory’s shearwaters** showing a crucial role for olfactory cues in homing after displacement [[22]](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.avrs.2025.100285)[[74]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-29919-0).
- Evidence that seabirds may rely more on olfaction offshore and more on visual/topographic cues near land [[74]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-29919-0)[[112]](https://www.bbc.com/news/science-environment-41077578)[[113]](https://www.smithsonianmag.com/smart-news/seabirds-use-their-sense-smell-navigate-open-water-180964674/).

The strongest caveat is taxonomic: olfactory maps are well supported in pigeons and some seabirds, but not yet generalizable to all migratory songbirds [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[74]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-29919-0)[[79]](https://www.nature.com/articles/srep37326)[[111]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4203629/).

### Visual landmarks and topographic knowledge

Visual landmarks clearly matter in **familiar areas**, along **coasts and rivers**, and probably during **final approach**, but evidence that birds use landmark-based cognitive maps for long-range navigation over unfamiliar terrain remains limited [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[114]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi)[[149]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-025-00543-8). Pigeons show stereotyped route following in familiar areas, and landscape edges can help with piloting and drift compensation [[50]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11512678/)[[54]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959438824000321)[[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z).

During migration, landmarks likely contribute most in three contexts:

1. **Piloting in familiar terrain** [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[114]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi)  
2. **Barrier following** along coasts, rivers, or mountain fronts [[37]](https://www.encyclopedie-environnement.org/en/life/orientation-migratory-birds/)[[178]](https://www.fws.gov/press-release/2018-04/new-study-uses-weather-radar-technology-identify-important-stopover-sites)[[188]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/retreat-detour-or-advance-understanding-movements-birds-confronting-gulf-mexico)  
3. **Final approach** to breeding or wintering sites [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[114]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi)

A key limit is evidentiary: reviews note that **no GPS-based study had yet confirmed a landmark-based cognitive map for birds** as of a recent synthesis, even though such a system remains biologically plausible [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/). So visual cues are unquestionably important, but current evidence supports them more strongly for **local guidance and route refinement** than for a fully general long-range map [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[114]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi).

### Infrasound and low-frequency environmental cues

Infrasound remains the least established cue class in this report. Some birds can detect very low-frequency sounds, and wandering albatross movements have been found to correlate with modeled **microbarom infrasound** fields, suggesting possible responsiveness [[115]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/ecology-and-evolution/articles/10.3389/fevo.2021.740027/full)[[195]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32237036/)[[197]](https://www.bmis-bycatch.org/system/files/zotero_attachments/library_1/U2C933RQ%20-%20Gillies%20et%20al.%20-%202023%20-%20Albatross%20movement%20suggests%20sensitivity%20to%20infraso.pdf). However, reviews emphasize that navigational use of infrasound as a map or beacon **remains untested or only correlational**, especially outside a few seabird systems [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[115]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/ecology-and-evolution/articles/10.3389/fevo.2021.740027/full)[[197]](https://www.bmis-bycatch.org/system/files/zotero_attachments/library_1/U2C933RQ%20-%20Gillies%20et%20al.%20-%202023%20-%20Albatross%20movement%20suggests%20sensitivity%20to%20infraso.pdf).

**Bottom line:** infrasound is biologically plausible and increasingly studied, but still far weaker as evidence than magnetic, celestial, olfactory, or landmark cues [[115]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/ecology-and-evolution/articles/10.3389/fevo.2021.740027/full)[[195]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32237036/)[[197]](https://www.bmis-bycatch.org/system/files/zotero_attachments/library_1/U2C933RQ%20-%20Gillies%20et%20al.%20-%202023%20-%20Albatross%20movement%20suggests%20sensitivity%20to%20infraso.pdf).

### Magnetic versus celestial versus olfactory versus visual cues

A direct comparison helps clarify why birds need multiple systems:

- **Magnetic cues** are the most **weather-robust directional reference**, especially when stars and Sun are obscured, and may sometimes contribute to positional estimation [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[37]](https://www.encyclopedie-environnement.org/en/life/orientation-migratory-birds/)[[68]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-30477-8).
- **Celestial cues** are often the most **geometrically precise directional references** when visible, but they are vulnerable to cloud, smoke, and light pollution [[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[37]](https://www.encyclopedie-environnement.org/en/life/orientation-migratory-birds/)[[65]](https://www.audubon.org/news/new-study-first-explore-how-wildfire-smoke-derails-bird-migration).
- **Olfactory cues** appear most valuable where **visual structure is sparse**, especially offshore, and where learned atmospheric gradients are informative [[74]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-29919-0)[[111]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4203629/).
- **Visual landmarks** are most useful when birds are already within a **familiar or topographically structured region**, or when coastlines, rivers, and habitat edges constrain movement [[50]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11512678/)[[114]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi)[[178]](https://www.fws.gov/press-release/2018-04/new-study-uses-weather-radar-technology-identify-important-stopover-sites)[[188]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/retreat-detour-or-advance-understanding-movements-birds-confronting-gulf-mexico).

In practice, the main distinction is not that one cue is “better” overall, but that different cues solve different informational problems at different spatial scales.

## How birds integrate multiple cues, recalibrate them, and resolve conflicts

The best-supported view is **multisensory integration with conditional weighting**, not a fixed universal hierarchy [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/)[[121]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574954125002274). Redundancy is crucial because the main cue systems fail under different conditions. Stars disappear under cloud; the Sun requires time compensation and visibility; magnetic signals can be noisy or disturbed; landmarks may be absent over ocean; odors are spatially patchy and taxon-specific [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[37]](https://www.encyclopedie-environnement.org/en/life/orientation-migratory-birds/)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d)[[74]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-29919-0)[[114]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi).

### General integration logic

1. **Use whichever directional cue is available and reliable now** [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[37]](https://www.encyclopedie-environnement.org/en/life/orientation-migratory-birds/).  
2. **Recalibrate compasses when cross-references are available**, especially at twilight [[42]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1513391113)[[44]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3049006/)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/).  
3. **Shift from inherited vector rules to learned maps and landmarks as experience accumulates** [[21]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12037376/)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/)[[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11285401/).  
4. **Weight cues differently by trip phase**: departure, en route flight, stopover, barrier crossing, and arrival do not require the same information [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[33]](https://www.bio.psy.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/bpsy/mam/content/papers/birdnav16.pdf)[[184]](https://restoreactscienceprogram.noaa.gov/projects/migratory-birds).  
5. **Compensate if one cue is missing, but persistent disruption occurs if key sensory processing is corrupted or if multiple cues fail simultaneously** [[10]](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13290)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/).

### Calibration and recalibration

Evidence from cue-conflict experiments suggests that the **horizon polarization pattern at sunrise or sunset** often calibrates the magnetic compass when birds can see the critical part of the sky [[42]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1513391113)[[44]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3049006/)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/). Once stars become visible, stellar reference may then be aligned with the calibrated magnetic system [[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/).

This helps explain why results differ across experiments: if a bird is tested in a funnel or setting that **blocks the lower horizon**, recalibration outcomes can differ from open-view conditions [[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/)[[158]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/behavioral-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnbeh.2016.00029/full)[[159]](https://lup.lub.lu.se/search/files/2905601/625386.pdf). Thus, apparent contradictions in “which compass dominates” often reflect **experimental geometry** and prior calibration history rather than simple species-level contradiction [72][124].

A further nuance is that some syntheses interpret the literature as showing **seasonal changes in cue weighting**: during the premigratory period celestial cues may be especially salient for calibrating the magnetic compass, whereas during active migration the magnetic compass may more often serve as the reference for celestial cues [124]. But the same review also noted that access to the **entire sunset sky**, especially the horizon region, can change outcomes, meaning some classic “seasonal hierarchy” results may partly reflect what birds were allowed to see rather than a fixed seasonal switch [124].

### Cue-conflict resolution model / decision tree

```text
If primary cue is available and calibrated:
    use it to set heading [1][72]

If primary cue is temporarily unavailable (e.g., overcast blocks stars/sun):
    switch to backup compass, often magnetic [1][37]

If twilight cues are available:
    recalibrate magnetic/celestial relationship [42][44][72]

If conflict occurs but one cue is clearly reliable and others are absent/degraded:
    short-term compensation likely [37][72]

If conflict persists and disturbs sensory transduction itself
(e.g., EM noise disrupts magnetic reception, intense ALAN attracts birds):
    persistent misorientation or disorientation more likely [10][28][64]

If experienced bird detects displacement:
    positional correction possible using map-like information [17][68][69]

If juvenile / first-time migrant lacks map:
    continues inherited heading more likely [69][70][152]
```

### Worked example: why overcast plus geomagnetic disturbance is worse than either alone

The 2023 Great Plains radar study provides a concrete case of cue interaction [122]:

1. **Baseline redundancy:** under clear conditions, a night migrant may cross-check stars and magnetic cues.
2. **Overcast alone:** stars are obscured, so the bird relies more heavily on magnetic information [1][37].
3. **Geomagnetic disturbance alone:** magnetic information becomes less reliable, but celestial cues may still provide orientation [20][122].
4. **Overcast + geomagnetic disturbance together:** both the backup and the primary night-time references are compromised at once.
5. **Observed population-level outcome:** migration intensity fell by **9–17%** during severe space weather, with stronger effects when geomagnetic disturbance coincided with overcast conditions; fall migrants also showed reduced effort flying against the wind, consistent with greater drift [[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10589677/).

This is a strong causal illustration of **redundancy breakdown**: disruption becomes larger when multiple cue systems fail simultaneously.

### Short-term cue conflict compensation vs persistent disruption

| Pattern | Typical cause | Example | Likely outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Short-term compensation** | One cue disappears but alternatives remain calibrated | Overcast sky removes stars, bird relies more on magnetic compass [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[37]](https://www.encyclopedie-environnement.org/en/life/orientation-migratory-birds/) | Maintains migration with limited disruption |
| **Recalibration** | Twilight allows cross-referencing among cues | Polarized light at horizon aligns magnetic and celestial systems [[42]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1513391113)[[44]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3049006/)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/) | Heading is updated |
| **Partial route correction** | Drift or moderate displacement with experience | Adults correct crosswind drift better than juveniles [[59]](https://lucris.lub.lu.se/ws/files/4386940/624645.pdf)[[85]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7661306/) | Some delay, but route can recover |
| **Persistent disruption** | Sensory processing itself is interfered with | EM noise abolishes magnetic orientation in robins [[10]](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13290)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d) | Continued disorientation until disturbance ends or cue switching succeeds |
| **Persistent attraction / trapping** | Artificial cue overrides normal orientation | Birds circle lights or concentrate in bright urban areas [[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/) | Delay, energy loss, collisions |
| **True navigation failure** | No effective positional map available | Juvenile starlings or sparrows continue inherited direction after displacement [[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/)[[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11285401/)[[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full) | Wrong wintering region or vagrancy |
