## Disturbances and disorientation factors

Disturbances affect migration by changing **cue availability**, **cue reliability**, **sensory processing**, or **ecological context** [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[20]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-26586-0)[[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z). The mechanism matters. A cloud layer that removes stars does not affect birds in the same way as broadband electromagnetic noise that may corrupt magnetic transduction, or as urban skyglow that can actively attract birds into hazardous airspace [[10]](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13290)[[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d).

### Disturbance mechanism table

| Disturbance | Cue blocked / distorted / interfered with | Affected phase(s) | Expected behavioral consequence | Strength of evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cloud cover / overcast** | Blocks Sun and stars; reduces twilight calibration quality [[37]](https://www.encyclopedie-environnement.org/en/life/orientation-migratory-birds/)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/) | Departure, en route | Increased reliance on magnetic compass; possible greater uncertainty if calibration poor [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[37]](https://www.encyclopedie-environnement.org/en/life/orientation-migratory-birds/) | Strong |
| **Crosswinds / storms** | Change ground track relative to heading; may force drift [[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z)[[59]](https://lucris.lub.lu.se/ws/files/4386940/624645.pdf)[[188]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/retreat-detour-or-advance-understanding-movements-birds-confronting-gulf-mexico) | En route, barrier crossing | Drift, partial or full compensation, detours, retreat from barriers [[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z)[[59]](https://lucris.lub.lu.se/ws/files/4386940/624645.pdf)[[188]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/retreat-detour-or-advance-understanding-movements-birds-confronting-gulf-mexico) | Strong |
| **Geomagnetic disturbance / space weather** | Distorts magnetic reliability [[20]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-26586-0)[[75]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9829733/)[[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10589677/) | En route, especially fall | Reduced migration intensity, more drift, increased vagrancy [[20]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-26586-0)[[75]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9829733/)[[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10589677/) | Strong macroecological support |
| **Wildfire smoke / haze** | Obscures visual cues, may alter olfactory context, raises physiological stress [[65]](https://www.audubon.org/news/new-study-first-explore-how-wildfire-smoke-derails-bird-migration)[[66]](https://ace-eco.org/vol17/iss2/art43/)[[91]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5522512/)[[140]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9286671/) | En route, stopover, barrier crossing | Delays, detours, energy deficit, reduced condition [[65]](https://www.audubon.org/news/new-study-first-explore-how-wildfire-smoke-derails-bird-migration)[[66]](https://ace-eco.org/vol17/iss2/art43/)[[140]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9286671/) | Moderate to strong |
| **Artificial light at night** | Masks celestial cues; attracts birds; alters circadian timing [[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[29]](https://www.un.org/en/un-chronicle/growing-effects-light-pollution-migratory-birds)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/) | Departure, en route, stopover, final approach | Attraction, circling, stopover redistribution, collisions, phenological shifts [[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[29]](https://www.un.org/en/un-chronicle/growing-effects-light-pollution-migratory-birds)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/) | Very strong |
| **Electromagnetic noise / radiofrequency noise** | Interferes with magnetic compass transduction [[10]](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13290)[[39]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6936046/)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d)[[133]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37399422/) | En route, orientation tasks | Disorientation or loss of magnetic compass use [[10]](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13290)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d) | Strong experimentally; field generalization still debated |
| **Habitat change / urbanization** | Removes or fragments stopover habitat; alters landmark context; adds light and collision hazards [[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[179]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44284-025-00388-7)[[184]](https://restoreactscienceprogram.noaa.gov/projects/migratory-birds)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/) | Stopover, barrier crossing, final approach | Redistribution into suboptimal sites, ecological traps, higher mortality [[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[179]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44284-025-00388-7)[[184]](https://restoreactscienceprogram.noaa.gov/projects/migratory-birds)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/) | Strong for habitat/stopover effects |
| **Climate change** | Alters timing, winds, stopover phenology, habitat geography [[60]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/detecting-mismatches-bird-migration-stopover-and-tree-phenology-response-changing)[[136]](https://www.youngfleshlab.com/assets/papers/Amaral-et-al-2025-DD.pdf)[[175]](https://ace-eco.org/vol19/iss2/art13/)[[190]](https://newsmediarelations.colostate.edu/2019/12/16/bird-migration-timing-skewed-by-climate-new-research-finds/) | All phases | Earlier or altered migration timing, mismatch with resources, route adjustments not always sufficient [[60]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/detecting-mismatches-bird-migration-stopover-and-tree-phenology-response-changing)[[136]](https://www.youngfleshlab.com/assets/papers/Amaral-et-al-2025-DD.pdf)[[190]](https://newsmediarelations.colostate.edu/2019/12/16/bird-migration-timing-skewed-by-climate-new-research-finds/) | Strong for timing/resource change; weaker for direct cue-mechanism disruption |

### Natural environmental variation vs anthropogenic disturbance

| Dimension | Natural variation | Anthropogenic disturbance |
|---|---|---|
| Typical mechanism | Changes cue availability or reliability | Can also corrupt sensory processing or create deceptive artificial cues |
| Examples | Clouds, storms, crosswinds, geomagnetic storms, smoke [[20]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-26586-0)[[37]](https://www.encyclopedie-environnement.org/en/life/orientation-migratory-birds/)[[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z)[[65]](https://www.audubon.org/news/new-study-first-explore-how-wildfire-smoke-derails-bird-migration) | ALAN, EM noise, glass/buildings, urbanization, habitat loss, climate change [[10]](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13290)[[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/) |
| Bird response | Cue switching, drift compensation, stopover delay, barrier retreat [[37]](https://www.encyclopedie-environnement.org/en/life/orientation-migratory-birds/)[[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z)[[188]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/retreat-detour-or-advance-understanding-movements-birds-confronting-gulf-mexico) | Attraction, disorientation, collision, stopover redistribution, chronic mismatch [[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/)[[190]](https://newsmediarelations.colostate.edu/2019/12/16/bird-migration-timing-skewed-by-climate-new-research-finds/) |
| Timescale | Often episodic | Can be chronic, spatially widespread, or increasing over time [[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[29]](https://www.un.org/en/un-chronicle/growing-effects-light-pollution-migratory-birds)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z) |
| Main uncertainty | Whether birds can compensate enough under extreme events [[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z)[[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10589677/) | How laboratory effects scale to free-flying populations and long-term fitness [[39]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6936046/)[[89]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4801848/)[[116]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11226503/) |

### Light pollution

Artificial light at night is now one of the best-documented navigation-relevant disturbances. It affects birds at **local**, **regional**, and **macroscale** levels [[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf). Strong evidence shows that migrants are attracted to lights, circle illuminated structures, and collide with buildings, especially during heavy migration and adverse weather [[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/).

Examples include:

- More than **40,000** dead birds recorded over decades at Chicago’s McCormick Place, with mortality highest when lights were on during large migration events [[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/).
- Long-term analysis estimated mortality there could be cut by about **60%** if lighted window area were reduced to historical minima [[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/).
- The 2023 continental stopover study used **over 10 million** remote-sensing observations, **more than 1 million** stopover locations, **49** predictors, and **2,500** models; in **over 70%** of the models, **skyglow** was a highly influential positive predictor of stopover density across the contiguous United States [[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z).
- Birds at about **500 m altitude** can still detect the skyglow of major cities, and stopover density increases near the brightest areas along some coasts [[178]](https://www.fws.gov/press-release/2018-04/new-study-uses-weather-radar-technology-identify-important-stopover-sites).

Mechanistically, ALAN does more than increase collisions. It can **mask stars**, **attract migrants into suboptimal stopover zones**, **alter departure or timing through circadian disruption**, and create **ecological traps** where birds are concentrated in hazardous landscapes [[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[29]](https://www.un.org/en/un-chronicle/growing-effects-light-pollution-migratory-birds)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z). The key causal point is that ALAN does not merely remove a natural cue; it adds a **novel, attractive cue-like signal** that can redirect movement itself [[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/).

### Electromagnetic noise

A landmark set of double-blind experiments showed that European robins near an urban campus lost normal magnetic orientation in unscreened huts but recovered it inside grounded, aluminum-screened huts that attenuated broadband electromagnetic noise; experimentally reintroducing the noise abolished orientation again [[10]](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13290)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d). This is among the clearest demonstrations that anthropogenic radiofrequency noise can disrupt magnetic compass use in birds [[10]](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13290)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d).

The ecological reach of this result is still debated. Some later laboratory and theoretical work supports radical-pair sensitivity to RF fields, while other studies question whether all behavioral effects match current biophysical models or how often wild birds encounter disruptive field conditions of the relevant frequencies and intensities [[39]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6936046/)[[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5582129/)[[133]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37399422/)[[139]](https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:21628613-f9de-446c-9d6d-91ae8f045c30/files/m4e6e225e4912c08e48c31d2e329f96db). Reviews note that experimental results differ between broadband and narrow-band exposures, and that metallic cage environments can themselves alter RF conditions, complicating comparisons across studies [[89]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4801848/)[[100]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6769297/)[[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5582129/).

### Storms, winds, and atmospheric conditions

Storms and wind fields are not just nuisances; they are major components of the migratory environment. Crosswinds can cause **drift**, to which birds respond with full compensation, partial compensation, adaptive drift, or retreat, depending on experience, route, and risk [[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z)[[59]](https://lucris.lub.lu.se/ws/files/4386940/624645.pdf)[[83]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3431116/)[[92]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8900403/).

A strong field example comes from Gulf of Mexico barrier studies: most tracked migrants approaching the Gulf did **not** immediately cross; about **75%** retreated inland or detoured instead, especially when fuel reserves were limited [[188]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/retreat-detour-or-advance-understanding-movements-birds-confronting-gulf-mexico). At continental scale, severe space weather events were associated with a **9–17% decrease** in migration intensity over the U.S. Great Plains and altered use of wind during fall migration, especially when overcast conditions coincided with geomagnetic disturbance [[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10589677/). A separate large-scale study linked geomagnetic disturbance to increased **vagrancy** using about **2 million captures** from **152** North American landbird species over **60 years**, reinforcing that magnetic unreliability can scale from individual orientation errors to range-wide distributional anomalies [[75]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9829733/).

### Habitat change and urbanization

Migration depends heavily on stopover and landfall habitat. Radar studies show stopover density is concentrated in **coastal forests**, **riparian corridors**, and other structurally favorable habitats [[177]](https://acjv.org/radar_study/FinalReport_La_Puma&Buler_2013.pdf)[[178]](https://www.fws.gov/press-release/2018-04/new-study-uses-weather-radar-technology-identify-important-stopover-sites). Habitat loss or fragmentation can therefore change where birds land, refuel, and depart.

The mechanism is not abstract. In a southeastern U.S. radar analysis, boosted regression tree models explained **62%** of deviance in fall and **35%** in spring; the most important predictors included **mean canopy height**, **NDVI**, **distance to coast**, and **elevation**, and the highest densities occurred near major water bodies, coastal forests, and forested riparian floodplains [[177]](https://acjv.org/radar_study/FinalReport_La_Puma&Buler_2013.pdf). Those same coastal areas often function as **departure points** for overwater flights in autumn and first landfall sites in spring [[177]](https://acjv.org/radar_study/FinalReport_La_Puma&Buler_2013.pdf). So habitat change can affect both **where birds decide to stop** and **whether they are willing to launch or land at all** [[177]](https://acjv.org/radar_study/FinalReport_La_Puma&Buler_2013.pdf)[[184]](https://restoreactscienceprogram.noaa.gov/projects/migratory-birds).

Recent large-scale analyses show that stopover hotspots are often disproportionately located in **metropolitan landscapes**, but this does not imply cities are beneficial overall [[179]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44284-025-00388-7)[[180]](https://source.colostate.edu/half-of-bird-migration-hot-spots-occur-in-metropolitan-areas-new-study-finds/). Cities may overlap historical flyway geography or provide irrigated green patches, especially in the West, yet they also combine lights, glass, vehicles, predators, and fragmented habitat [[179]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44284-025-00388-7)[[180]](https://source.colostate.edu/half-of-bird-migration-hot-spots-occur-in-metropolitan-areas-new-study-finds/). Across the U.S., skyglow is a major positive predictor of stopover density, suggesting that urbanization can actively reshape the geography of stopover rather than merely add mortality after birds arrive [[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z). Institutional syntheses further report that migrants favor **hardwood forest patches within developed landscapes**, especially where arthropod and fruit resources are high, and that densities are especially high in coastal areas [[178]](https://www.fws.gov/press-release/2018-04/new-study-uses-weather-radar-technology-identify-important-stopover-sites). This means urbanization changes navigation not only by hazard, but by altering the **distribution and salience of residual habitat targets** [[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[178]](https://www.fws.gov/press-release/2018-04/new-study-uses-weather-radar-technology-identify-important-stopover-sites).

Habitat loss at major stopover systems such as the Yellow Sea has been linked to redistribution and intensified competition, with tidal-flat area declining by more than one-third in one long-term analysis [[182]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0006320722001008). Along the northern Gulf coast, habitat quantity and quality directly affect the first landfall and last departure zones for many migrants [[184]](https://restoreactscienceprogram.noaa.gov/projects/migratory-birds).

### Climate change

Climate change is clearly affecting migration, but it usually does so by changing **timing**, **winds**, **resource phenology**, and **stopover geography**, rather than by directly altering a bird’s compass sense [[60]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/detecting-mismatches-bird-migration-stopover-and-tree-phenology-response-changing)[[136]](https://www.youngfleshlab.com/assets/papers/Amaral-et-al-2025-DD.pdf)[[175]](https://ace-eco.org/vol19/iss2/art13/)[[190]](https://newsmediarelations.colostate.edu/2019/12/16/bird-migration-timing-skewed-by-climate-new-research-finds/).

Key findings include:

- Continental radar data show earlier spring passage over the last two decades, strongest where warming is greatest [[189]](https://asri.org/news-events/articles-2019-02/study-finds-bird-migration-timing-skewed-by-climate-change.html)[[190]](https://newsmediarelations.colostate.edu/2019/12/16/bird-migration-timing-skewed-by-climate-new-research-finds/).
- Birds can speed migration in years with earlier or faster spring green-up, but often **do not fully compensate** for advancing resource phenology [[136]](https://www.youngfleshlab.com/assets/papers/Amaral-et-al-2025-DD.pdf).
- Stopover mismatch can arise when migrant abundance and plant or insect phenology shift differently across habitats and years [[60]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/detecting-mismatches-bird-migration-stopover-and-tree-phenology-response-changing).
- Climate effects are spatially heterogeneous, meaning birds encounter different cue and resource shifts along different parts of the route [[135]](https://phys.org/news/2024-01-mismatched-climate-bird-migration.html)[[191]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12513722/).

Thus, the main navigation-relevant climate threat is often **cue mismatch**: the environmental conditions that once made a given timing rule or route reliable are no longer synchronized across the annual cycle [[175]](https://ace-eco.org/vol19/iss2/art13/)[[190]](https://newsmediarelations.colostate.edu/2019/12/16/bird-migration-timing-skewed-by-climate-new-research-finds/)[[191]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12513722/).

## Species differences, migration mode, life stage, and ecological context

No single cue combination fits all migratory birds [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[121]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574954125002274). The strongest comparative pattern is not “species A uses cue X, species B uses cue Y,” but that cue weighting changes with **experience**, **time of day**, **route structure**, and **habitat context** [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[21]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12037376/)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/).

### Species / life-stage / migration-mode comparison table

| Comparison | Typical pattern | Representative evidence |
|---|---|---|
| **Juveniles vs adults** | Juveniles often rely more on inherited vector orientation; adults more often compensate for displacement and wind drift [[21]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12037376/)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/)[[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11285401/)[[84]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9581414/) | Adult but not juvenile white-crowned sparrows compensated after 3,700 km displacement [[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/); adult but not juvenile starlings compensated in Perdeck’s study [[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11285401/)[[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full) |
| **First-time vs experienced migrants** | Experience improves route correction, map use, stopover efficiency, and drift compensation [[21]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12037376/)[[59]](https://lucris.lub.lu.se/ws/files/4386940/624645.pdf)[[84]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9581414/) | Reed warbler magnetic map effects and raptor drift compensation are stronger in experienced birds [[17]](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-30477-8)[[59]](https://lucris.lub.lu.se/ws/files/4386940/624645.pdf)[[102]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1664630/) |
| **Day migrants vs night migrants** | Day migrants can use Sun and landmarks more continuously; night migrants rely more on stars, magnetic compass, and twilight calibration [[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/)[[80]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9532420/)[[81]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5989362/) | Route models and experiments indicate different cue geometries and strengths for day vs night migration [[80]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9532420/)[[81]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5989362/) |
| **Short-distance vs long-distance migrants** | Long-distance migrants face stronger pressure for precise barrier handling, timing, and compensatory navigation [[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z)[[80]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9532420/)[[184]](https://restoreactscienceprogram.noaa.gov/projects/migratory-birds) | Cuckoos, godwits, and reed warblers show flexible correction or strong compensation over large scales [[16]](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep16402)[[18]](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2024.1363)[[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z) |
| **Pelagic vs continental migrants** | Pelagic migrants often rely more on olfaction, wind fields, and large-scale cue integration where landmarks are sparse [[22]](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.avrs.2025.100285)[[74]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-29919-0)[[197]](https://www.bmis-bycatch.org/system/files/zotero_attachments/library_1/U2C933RQ%20-%20Gillies%20et%20al.%20-%202023%20-%20Albatross%20movement%20suggests%20sensitivity%20to%20infraso.pdf) | Cory’s shearwaters and wandering albatrosses show offshore cue specializations [[22]](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.avrs.2025.100285)[[74]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-29919-0)[[197]](https://www.bmis-bycatch.org/system/files/zotero_attachments/library_1/U2C933RQ%20-%20Gillies%20et%20al.%20-%202023%20-%20Albatross%20movement%20suggests%20sensitivity%20to%20infraso.pdf) |
| **Social vs solitary migrants** | Social learning can substitute partly for individual map knowledge in some species [[71]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5558239/)[[73]](https://academics.lmu.edu/media/lmuacademics/cures/urbanecolab/module11/Migration%20and%20Navigation%20in%20Birds%20(Watts%202017).pdf) | Juvenile lesser spotted eagles benefit from moving during adult migration periods [[71]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5558239/) |

### Juveniles and first-time migrants

The classic picture remains broadly valid: many juveniles can migrate successfully with an **inherited program**, but without adult-like positional navigation [[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full)[[153]](https://www.internationalornithology.org/PROCEEDINGS_Durban/Symposium/S17/S17.1.htm). Displacement studies are especially revealing: juveniles often keep going in the seasonally correct direction, while adults reorient toward the appropriate goal region [[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/)[[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11285401/)[[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full).

Still, the old dichotomy is now seen as too simple. Recent reviews argue that some first-time migrants may possess limited location control, “beacons,” or signpost-like information rather than a full map or a pure vector only [[21]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12037376/)[[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full). The best-supported conclusion is therefore not “juveniles have no navigation,” but that **their navigation is typically less corrective and less map-dependent than adult navigation** [[21]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12037376/)[[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full).

### Experienced adults

Adults usually show greater **site fidelity**, **displacement compensation**, **route correction**, and **wind handling** [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[59]](https://lucris.lub.lu.se/ws/files/4386940/624645.pdf)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/). In raptors, adults compensate for crosswinds far better than juveniles [[59]](https://lucris.lub.lu.se/ws/files/4386940/624645.pdf). In solitary long-distance migrants such as cuckoos and some songbirds, adults can rejoin normal routes after large experimental displacement [[16]](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep16402)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/).

But adult experience does not guarantee successful compensation in every species or context. The white-throated sparrow example shows that even experienced birds may fail to reveal a clear goalward map response under experimental release conditions [[79]](https://www.nature.com/articles/srep37326). So “adult” is best treated as increasing the probability of map-based correction, not as guaranteeing it.

### Day migrants vs night migrants

- **Day migrants** can make more continuous use of the Sun and visible landmarks, and social information is often more available [[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[71]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5558239/)[[81]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5989362/).
- **Night migrants** use the star compass when skies are clear, often calibrate at twilight, and rely heavily on the magnetic compass when celestial cues are unavailable [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/)[[80]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9532420/).

These are trends, not absolute rules. Even day migrants can use magnetic cues, and many night migrants also use topography or coastlines during stopover or landfall [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z)[[178]](https://www.fws.gov/press-release/2018-04/new-study-uses-weather-radar-technology-identify-important-stopover-sites).

### Ecological context matters

- **Coasts and lakefronts** naturally concentrate migrants and can interact strongly with urban lights [[177]](https://acjv.org/radar_study/FinalReport_La_Puma&Buler_2013.pdf)[[178]](https://www.fws.gov/press-release/2018-04/new-study-uses-weather-radar-technology-identify-important-stopover-sites)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/).
- **Barrier crossings** over ocean or desert magnify the cost of drift and poor stopover decisions [[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z)[[184]](https://restoreactscienceprogram.noaa.gov/projects/migratory-birds)[[188]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/retreat-detour-or-advance-understanding-movements-birds-confronting-gulf-mexico).
- **Familiar areas** favor landmark and route-memory use [[50]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11512678/)[[114]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi).
- **Open ocean** likely increases the value of olfactory, atmospheric, and possibly low-frequency environmental cues [[74]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-29919-0)[[115]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/ecology-and-evolution/articles/10.3389/fevo.2021.740027/full)[[197]](https://www.bmis-bycatch.org/system/files/zotero_attachments/library_1/U2C933RQ%20-%20Gillies%20et%20al.%20-%202023%20-%20Albatross%20movement%20suggests%20sensitivity%20to%20infraso.pdf).

## Key experiments, tracking evidence, historical milestones, and competing interpretations

### How to read the evidence

The avian navigation literature uses different methods that answer different questions:

- **Orientation cages / funnels** offer strong **causal control** over cues but lower ecological realism [[3]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5513225/)[[116]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11226503/).
- **Clock-shift and cue-manipulation experiments** isolate specific compass mechanisms [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[11]](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF01952166)[[13]](https://doi.org/10.1038/364523a0).
- **Displacement and virtual displacement studies** are stronger tests of **map sense** [[17]](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-30477-8)[[18]](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2024.1363)[[68]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-30477-8)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/).
- **Tracking, radar, and telemetry** show what happens in real migrations, but often provide weaker causal isolation unless paired with environmental or experimental variation [[16]](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep16402)[[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10589677/)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z).

A robust synthesis therefore requires combining these evidence types rather than treating any one method as sufficient.

### Annotated timeline of landmark studies and advances

- **1940s** — Large displacement studies begin to separate simple orientation from map-based navigation; Rüppell’s hooded crow work suggests some migrants do not compensate after displacement [[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full).
- **Late 1940s–1950s** — **Gustav Kramer** formalizes the **map-and-compass** framework and demonstrates sun-compass use in orientation cages [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[151]](http://oops.uni-oldenburg.de/1168/1/kislon11.pdf).
- **1958** — **Perdeck’s starling displacement** becomes the classic adult-vs-juvenile evidence for learned navigation versus inherited vector orientation [[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11285401/)[[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full).
- **1960s** — **Emlen funnel** methods standardize laboratory orientation assays [[3]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5513225/)[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emlen_funnel).
- **1960s–1970s** — **Emlen’s planetarium experiments** show that the star compass is learned from celestial rotation [[7]](https://www.shaverscreek.org/2015/06/09/marvel-of-migration-navigation-and-orientation/)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[164]](https://nationalzoo.si.edu/migratory-birds/news/stellar-migrant).
- **1960s–1970s** — **Magnetic orientation** demonstrated in migratory birds such as European robins [[10]](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13290)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/).
- **1970s onward** — **Papi/Gagliardo olfactory work** establishes strong evidence for olfactory maps in pigeons and later seabirds [[53]](https://fr.scribd.com/document/376178988/Gagliardo-2013-Forty-years-of-bird-olfactory-navigation-pdf)[[170]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23720797/)[[171]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/1838523/).
- **1980s–1990s** — Polarized-light and cue-conflict experiments reveal cross-calibration among compasses [[13]](https://doi.org/10.1038/364523a0)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/)[[158]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/behavioral-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnbeh.2016.00029/full).
- **2000s** — Telemetry and tracking begin linking lab mechanisms to free-flying migrants, including daily twilight recalibration and displacement correction [[4]](https://journals.ansfoundation.org/index.php/jans/article/view/2684)[[15]](http://dx.doi.org/doi:10.1038/srep33805)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/).
- **2010s** — **Virtual magnetic displacement** and trigeminal manipulations in reed warblers strengthen evidence for magnetic positional information [[17]](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-30477-8)[[68]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-30477-8)[[157]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6086908/).
- **2014** — Nature paper shows **anthropogenic electromagnetic noise** can disrupt magnetic orientation [[10]](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13290)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d).
- **2020s** — Continental radar, automated telemetry, and multi-sensor analyses reveal migration-wide effects of **space weather**, **light pollution**, **urbanization**, and **climate-linked timing shifts** [[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10589677/)[[123]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12309642/)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[179]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44284-025-00388-7)[[190]](https://newsmediarelations.colostate.edu/2019/12/16/bird-migration-timing-skewed-by-climate-new-research-finds/).
- **2024–2026** — Advances in cryptochrome biophysics, virtual-displacement critique, and a 3D migratory-bird brain atlas sharpen both mechanistic opportunity and uncertainty [[126]](https://www.ucl.ac.uk/news/2026/apr/scientists-map-migratory-bird-brain)[[127]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42013800/)[[128]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42003-023-04530-w)[[131]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-55124-x)[[134]](https://ifv-vogelwarte.de/en/research/projects/cryptochrome-adaptive-evolution-and-loss-of-a-putative-magnetoreceptor-in-birds).

## Debates, uncertainties, and open research questions

### Debates and uncertainty matrix

| Hypothesis / issue | Supporting evidence | Conflicting evidence / limit | Current consensus |
|---|---|---|---|

### What the strongest debates are actually about

Several controversies are narrower than they first appear:

- The dispute over the **magnetic compass** is **not** mainly about whether birds have one. That point is strongly supported. The dispute is about **molecular identity**, **signal transduction**, and how to reconcile behavioral RF sensitivity with biophysical models [[39]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6936046/)[[100]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6769297/)[[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5582129/)[[131]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-55124-x)[[139]](https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:21628613-f9de-446c-9d6d-91ae8f045c30/files/m4e6e225e4912c08e48c31d2e329f96db).
- The dispute over the **magnetic map** is about **generality** and **coordinate structure**. Reed warblers provide compelling evidence, but they do not automatically imply a universal map across all migratory birds [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[18]](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2024.1363)[[68]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-30477-8)[[79]](https://www.nature.com/articles/srep37326).
- The dispute over **landmarks** is not whether birds use them—they clearly do—but whether birds possess a **general long-range landmark-based cognitive map** of unfamiliar terrain. Current evidence remains limited [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[114]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi).
- The dispute over **disturbance** is often a scaling problem: laboratory evidence can be causal but local, whereas radar and collision evidence can be large-scale but more observational [[10]](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13290)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d)[[122]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10589677/)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z).

### Main open questions

1. **What exactly is the sensory receptor for positional magnetic information?** Trigeminal involvement is supported, but receptor cells and physical transduction remain unresolved [[17]](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-30477-8)[[49]](https://www.sinnesphysiologie.de/download/birdrev05.pdf)[[100]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6769297/)[[106]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3552369/).
2. **How general are magnetic map mechanisms across birds?** Reed warblers are compelling; general migratory universality is not demonstrated [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[18]](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2024.1363)[[68]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-30477-8)[[79]](https://www.nature.com/articles/srep37326).
3. **How mapless are first-time migrants?** The classic vector-only model is useful, but recent reviews suggest some juveniles may possess more innate positional structure than previously assumed [[21]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12037376/)[[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full).
4. **How should laboratory cue-conflict results be extrapolated to free flight?** Cage studies isolate mechanisms, but free-flying birds can access more cues and may behave differently [[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/)[[108]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9604362/)[[116]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11226503/).
5. **How much of disturbance impact reflects cue corruption versus ecological trap formation?** ALAN and urbanization can alter cue perception directly and also redistribute birds into hazardous or low-quality habitats [[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[179]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44284-025-00388-7)[[185]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8214683/).
6. **Can climate change degrade navigation directly, or mainly through timing and habitat mismatch?** Current evidence is strongest for the latter [[60]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/detecting-mismatches-bird-migration-stopover-and-tree-phenology-response-changing)[[175]](https://ace-eco.org/vol19/iss2/art13/)[[190]](https://newsmediarelations.colostate.edu/2019/12/16/bird-migration-timing-skewed-by-climate-new-research-finds/)[[191]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12513722/).

## Ecological implications of navigation success and failure

Navigation is not only a sensory-biology problem; it shapes population ecology.

### Route fidelity and migratory connectivity

Adults often return with high precision to breeding and nonbreeding regions, reinforcing **migratory connectivity** between seasonal populations [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[168]](https://research.bangor.ac.uk/en/projects/unravelling-the-map-and-compass-in-bird-navigation/). That fidelity depends on successfully combining long-range guidance with local approach cues, and it helps structure population-specific routes and conservation risk [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[175]](https://ace-eco.org/vol19/iss2/art13/).

### Stopover dependence

Most migration time is spent at **stopover**, not in continuous flight, and stopover habitat quality strongly affects migration duration and condition [[165]](https://www.fs.usda.gov/psw/publications/documents/psw_gtr191/psw_gtr191_0684-0689_moore.pdf)[[184]](https://restoreactscienceprogram.noaa.gov/projects/migratory-birds). Navigation failure can therefore mean not only “flying the wrong way,” but also landing in poor habitat, missing refueling windows, or being drawn into ecological traps near cities [[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[183]](https://etd.ohiolink.edu/acprod/odb_etd/ws/send_file/send)[[184]](https://restoreactscienceprogram.noaa.gov/projects/migratory-birds).

### Barrier crossing risk

Errors are especially costly near major barriers such as oceans, deserts, or large lakes. Tracked migrants often retreat or detour rather than commit to crossing under poor conditions, showing that migration success depends on dynamic environmental assessment, not just compass fidelity [[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z)[[188]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/retreat-detour-or-advance-understanding-movements-birds-confronting-gulf-mexico). The Gulf example is especially instructive: when approximately **75%** of approaching birds retreat or detour rather than cross, the relevant “navigation” behavior is not simply compass holding but risk-sensitive decision-making about when a route is viable [[188]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/retreat-detour-or-advance-understanding-movements-birds-confronting-gulf-mexico).

### Vagrancy and misplacement

Geomagnetic disturbance and other cue failures can increase **vagrancy**, producing birds far outside normal ranges [[20]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-26586-0)[[75]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9829733/)[[172]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/geomagnetic-disturbances-and-wrong-way-bird-migrations/). That matters ecologically because repeated misplacement can reduce survival, alter community composition at stopovers, and complicate range shifts under changing climate [[20]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-26586-0)[[75]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9829733/).

### Energetic cost and survival

Smoke, detours, circling around lights, and delayed departures all increase energy expenditure. In Tule geese, smoke-driven route changes more than doubled migration duration and substantially increased flight distance and energetic deficit [[140]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9286671/)[[141]](https://eros.usgs.gov/doi-remote-sensing-activities/2022/usgs/wildfire-and-smoke-affect-bird-migration-western-north-america). Similar energetic costs likely underlie many urban-light and barrier-retreat effects [[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[188]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/retreat-detour-or-advance-understanding-movements-birds-confronting-gulf-mexico). The mechanism is straightforward: when navigation is disrupted, birds do not merely lose geometric precision; they also spend more time aloft, burn more fuel, and may reach stopovers or breeding areas in poorer condition [[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[140]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9286671/)[[188]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/retreat-detour-or-advance-understanding-movements-birds-confronting-gulf-mexico).

### Timing and breeding consequences

If birds arrive too early or too late relative to food, plant phenology, or nesting conditions, fitness can decline. Climate change increasingly threatens migration by weakening the historical match between internal timing programs and external resource phenology [[60]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/detecting-mismatches-bird-migration-stopover-and-tree-phenology-response-changing)[[136]](https://www.youngfleshlab.com/assets/papers/Amaral-et-al-2025-DD.pdf)[[175]](https://ace-eco.org/vol19/iss2/art13/)[[191]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12513722/).

## Conclusion

Migratory birds achieve precise migration by solving **two linked but distinct problems**. They determine **direction** mainly with **compass systems**—the Sun, stars, Earth’s magnetic field, and polarized light-based calibration. They determine **location**, when needed, with more variable and species-specific **map-like systems** that can involve magnetic signatures, olfactory information, learned landmarks, and route memory [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[33]](https://www.bio.psy.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/bpsy/mam/content/papers/birdnav16.pdf)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[50]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11512678/)[[68]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-30477-8)[[98]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7618438/).

The strongest current consensus is that birds are **multisensory navigators**. They recalibrate cues at twilight, switch among cue types as weather and visibility change, and rely increasingly on learned information with experience. First-time migrants often depend more on inherited vector programs; experienced adults more often show displacement compensation, route correction, and precise site fidelity [[21]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12037376/)[[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2084305/)[[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11285401/)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/)[[152]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2025.1562569/full).

The main cues are:
- **Magnetic field / magnetoreception**: strong evidence for a light-dependent magnetic compass; positional magnetic use supported in some species but still debated in general [[17]](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-30477-8)[[32]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12365788/)[[39]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6936046/)[[100]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6769297/).
- **Sun compass**: robust time-compensated directional system [[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[11]](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF01952166)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/).
- **Star compass**: learned from celestial rotation, crucial for many night migrants [[7]](https://www.shaverscreek.org/2015/06/09/marvel-of-migration-navigation-and-orientation/)[[35]](https://www.allaboutbirds.org/news/the-basics-migration-navigation/)[[164]](https://nationalzoo.si.edu/migratory-birds/news/stellar-migrant).
- **Polarized light**: especially important for calibration at sunrise and sunset [[13]](https://doi.org/10.1038/364523a0)[[42]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1513391113)[[72]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4763052/).
- **Visual landmarks**: important for local guidance, familiar areas, coasts, and final approach [[50]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11512678/)[[114]](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi)[[178]](https://www.fws.gov/press-release/2018-04/new-study-uses-weather-radar-technology-identify-important-stopover-sites).
- **Olfactory cues**: strong map evidence in pigeons and some seabirds, less universal elsewhere [[22]](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.avrs.2025.100285)[[53]](https://fr.scribd.com/document/376178988/Gagliardo-2013-Forty-years-of-bird-olfactory-navigation-pdf)[[74]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-018-29919-0)[[170]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23720797/).
- **Wind/weather cues**: shape route timing, drift, stopovers, and barrier decisions throughout migration [[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40462-022-00310-z)[[59]](https://lucris.lub.lu.se/ws/files/4386940/624645.pdf)[[188]](https://www.usgs.gov/publications/retreat-detour-or-advance-understanding-movements-birds-confronting-gulf-mexico).
- **Infrasound/low-frequency cues**: plausible and promising, but still much weaker than the other cue systems [[115]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/ecology-and-evolution/articles/10.3389/fevo.2021.740027/full)[[197]](https://www.bmis-bycatch.org/system/files/zotero_attachments/library_1/U2C933RQ%20-%20Gillies%20et%20al.%20-%202023%20-%20Albatross%20movement%20suggests%20sensitivity%20to%20infraso.pdf).

What disturbs navigation depends on mechanism. **Clouds, storms, smoke, and geomagnetic disturbance** alter cue availability or reliability; **light pollution** attracts and disorients birds and reshapes stopover geography; **electromagnetic noise** can interfere with magnetic orientation; **habitat change and urbanization** remove or distort critical stopover and approach environments; and **climate change** increasingly disrupts the timing and ecological reliability of migration routes [[20]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-26586-0)[[28]](https://lightsoutheartland.org/images/articles/Burt_TREE_2023EcolandEvolrevlighteffectsonmigration2023.pdf)[[64]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Anthropogenic-electromagnetic-noise-disrupts-in-a-Engels-Schneider/41364722854d06dfd21befc0d2c55f75c0cca72d)[[176]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-43046-z)[[179]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44284-025-00388-7)[[190]](https://newsmediarelations.colostate.edu/2019/12/16/bird-migration-timing-skewed-by-climate-new-research-finds/).

So the most accurate answer is: birds do not navigate migration with one master sense. They achieve precision through **redundant compass systems, map-like positional information, internal timing, learning, and ecological decision-making across trip phases**. Their success depends not only on intact sensory mechanisms, but also on an environment whose cues remain **available, reliable, and biologically meaningful** [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11900352/)[[2]](https://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/dam/sdc/pds/ceqa/JVR/AdminRecord/IncorporatedByReference/Section-2-3---Biological-Resources-References/Emlen,%20S.T.%201975_Migration%20Orientation%20and%20Navigation.pdf)[[121]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574954125002274).
